result:
# The Texture of Tuesday

I want to write about Tuesdays.

Not because Tuesdays are interesting—they are famously, almost professionally, uninteresting—but because I think there's something worth excavating in that specific variety of mundane. Mondays get all the dread. Fridays get the relief and the loosened collars and the particular amber light that seems to exist only on Friday afternoons in autumn. Wednesdays get the hump. Saturdays and Sundays are too loaded with expectation to be themselves. But Tuesdays just sit there, grey and functional, like a good utility knife that nobody notices until they need it.

There's a word I keep returning to: *texture*. It's become one of those words that intellectuals deploy too freely, the way *liminal* got exhausted in the early 2010s and *nuanced* has been worn down to a nub. But I want to use it anyway, carefully, because I think it's the right word for what I'm trying to get at. Texture isn't appearance. It's not the color of something or its shape. Texture is what you feel when you run your hand across it. It's the grain of a thing. And Tuesdays, I would argue, have a very particular grain—slightly rough, slightly workmanlike, resistant to romanticism.

Here's what I actually think is going on with Tuesdays, and why I find them useful to think about:

Tuesdays expose the armature of ordinary life. They are the days when you cannot pretend that existence is anything other than what it actually is, which is mostly just showing up and doing the next thing. On a Monday you still have the dramatic narrative of the week's beginning to sustain you. On a Friday you have the finish line. On a Tuesday you have nothing but the work itself, the errand itself, the meal itself. Tuesday strips away the story you tell about the week and leaves you with the week's raw material. And that raw material, most of the time, is neither tragic nor triumphant. It is just real.

I have been thinking lately—and this is connected, I promise—about the difference between experiences and the memory of experiences. There's a well-known idea, associated with the psychologist Daniel Kahneman, that the *experiencing self* and the *remembering self* are essentially two different entities with two different agendas. The experiencing self lives in real-time, moment to moment. The remembering self constructs narratives and is deeply susceptible to what Kahneman calls the *peak-end rule*: you tend to remember an experience based on its most intense moment and its final moment, with almost no regard for duration. A long, mildly pleasant vacation can be remembered worse than a short, intense one. A painful medical procedure is remembered as worse if the final moments are bad, even if the overall duration of pain was less.

What this means is that the remembering self is a ruthless editor. It cuts all the Tuesdays. It keeps the peaks—the wedding, the accident, the revelation, the achievement—and it keeps the endings. The long middle, the ordinary fabric of a life, gets compressed into nothing in the retrospective account. Decades of Tuesday afternoons vanish. What you remember of a year, looking back, might amount to six or eight scenes, none of which took place on a Tuesday.

I find this genuinely vertiginous to think about. It means that the largest portion of a life—measured by hours, by cells dividing and dying, by heartbeats, by the sheer quantity of experience passing through consciousness—is precisely the portion that leaves the least trace in memory. The Tuesdays are where you *lived*, in the most literal metabolic sense, but they are not where you *remember living*. The life and the account of the life diverge enormously.

And here's where I want to make a possibly strange argument: I think this is one of the most important things to understand about being alive, and I think most people understand it only intermittently, in flashes, and then immediately forget it again.

---

When I think about what it means to pay attention, I keep coming back to a few writers who seem to have cracked something open about this problem.

Marilynne Robinson writes in *Gilead*—a novel narrated by an elderly preacher writing letters to his young son, letters the son will read after the father is dead—about the sheer improbability and richness of ordinary things. The narrator, John Ames, is a man who has lived a quiet life in a small Iowa town. By conventional metrics, not much has happened to him. But what Robinson does, over and over, is locate in Ames a kind of dazzled attention to the texture of the ordinary that reads less like contentment and more like astonishment. He watches the light in the morning. He watches his son play. He notices the way water looks. And what you feel, reading him, is not that he's a man who has accepted a small life, but that he has understood something about scale that most people miss—that there is no such thing as a small life when you're actually inside it, when you're actually present to it.

There's a sentence in that book that I think about often: *"I have lived my life on the prairie and a few times I have seen it dazzle."* That word *dazzle* does so much work. It's not claiming transcendence. It's not claiming that the prairie is secretly spectacular. It's acknowledging the rarity of the moments when ordinary things disclose their depth, and treating that rarity not as a deprivation but as a gift—because the disclosure is real, even if it doesn't happen every day.

This is different from mindfulness, which I am slightly suspicious of as a framework, not because presence is a bad idea but because the wellness-industrial version of it tends to treat the present moment as something to be accessed, as though you can download the present through the right breathing technique. What Robinson is describing, through Ames, is something less procedural. It's more like a quality of character, built over time, a long faithfulness to the particular. You have to have been watching the light in the morning for decades before it dazzles you. The dazzle is partly a reward for consistency of attention.

---

Let me go somewhere else for a minute.

I have been reading about deep time, the geological and cosmological variety, and I find it does something strange to my sense of the present. Deep time is the time of rock strata, of species rising and falling, of continents moving, of stars forming out of the collapse of earlier stars' remnants. The Earth is approximately 4.5 billion years old. Complex animal life is perhaps 600 million years old. Homo sapiens have existed for somewhere between 200,000 and 300,000 years. Writing, the technology that allows me to be having this particular kind of thought, is about 5,000 years old.

When you hold those numbers in your head and think about your Tuesday, the Tuesday does one of two things: it either becomes completely meaningless, an absurd blip in an indifferent expanse, or it becomes almost unbearably precious. I find that it does the second, though I acknowledge this might be a temperamental preference rather than a logical conclusion.

Here's the logic as I experience it: the universe didn't have Tuesdays for almost all of its existence. Tuesdays required stars to synthesize carbon, planets to form, life to emerge, animals to develop nervous systems complex enough to experience duration, and then one particular animal to develop language and culture and a seven-day week for entirely contingent historical reasons—the Babylonians, the planets visible to the naked eye, the transmission through Jewish tradition, the Roman calendar, and so on. The Tuesday is a staggeringly improbable cultural artifact, and my experience of it is a staggeringly improbable neurological event. The fact that Tuesday feels like nothing, like just another day, doesn't mean it is nothing. It means I have become habituated, which is a different thing.

There's a concept in neuroscience and psychology called *hedonic adaptation*: the tendency of humans to return to a roughly stable level of emotional response regardless of what happens to them. Lottery winners, a year after their win, report roughly the same levels of happiness as before. People who become paralyzed below the waist report, after a period of adjustment, roughly similar levels of life satisfaction as those who are not paralyzed. This is both impressive and slightly melancholy. It demonstrates the resilience of the human organism. It also demonstrates that we are bad at holding onto the understanding of how good or how rare things are. We adapt. We normalize. We convert the remarkable into the unremarkable, because the nervous system requires us to—to keep doing that thing where we get up and do the next thing.

So the very adaptation that makes life livable also makes it harder to see. The mechanism of survival obscures the value of the thing being survived.

---

I want to talk about boredom, which is Tuesday's companion and unofficial mascot.

Boredom has a bad reputation that I think is largely undeserved. Modern life has declared essentially total war on boredom. Every idle moment is now a slot that can be filled with content—a phone pulled from a pocket, a podcast queued up, a scroll through whatever image-delivery system you prefer. The assault on boredom has been so successful that many people now feel genuine distress when forced to sit with nothing, at a bus stop or in a waiting room, without any external stimulation. The silence has become intolerable because we have trained ourselves to treat it as a problem.

But boredom, properly understood, is not a problem. It's a signal. It's the mind's way of saying: *there is nothing currently demanding your attention, which means your attention is available.* Boredom is cognitive slack. And cognitive slack is where things happen—where memories consolidate, where problems solve themselves in the background, where desires clarify, where you figure out what you actually want as opposed to what the nearest screen is suggesting you might want.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi—the psychologist who developed the theory of *flow*—described a kind of unhappiness that he observed in people who could not tolerate the absence of stimulation. He called it *psychic entropy*: a disorder in consciousness that occurs when attention has nothing to organize it. But I think his prescription, which was essentially to fill consciousness with optimally challenging activities to achieve flow states, is only part of the answer. The other part is learning to sit with the entropy, to be in it without immediately fleeing. Because entropy isn't only disorder—it's also a kind of fertile chaos, a clearing, the compost of the mind.

When I am bored—genuinely, uncomfortably bored—strange things happen. Associations form that wouldn't form if I were directed. I start noticing things I wasn't looking for. The quality of light in a room. The way someone across a café holds their cup. A word that seems suddenly wrong for what it's supposed to mean. These are not useful thoughts, necessarily. They are not productive. But they are *mine*, in a way that the content I consume is not mine, and they feel, however trivially, like discoveries.

---

There's a Japanese aesthetic concept I keep circling back to: *mono no aware*. It translates roughly as *the pathos of things*, or sometimes *an empathy toward things*, and it describes a gentle, slightly melancholy appreciation for the transience of things. It's often associated with cherry blossoms—beautiful, yes, but also there for only a week or two before they fall. The beauty and the transience are inseparable in this framework. You can't appreciate the one without being aware of the other. The falling is part of the beauty.

I think this is a more honest account of value than the Western tendency to treat beauty and permanence as allies, to want the beautiful thing to last, to be frustrated when it doesn't. Mono no aware accepts the structure of reality—that things arise and pass, that the arising and the passing are one movement—and finds in that structure not tragedy but something tenderer and more complex. Not happiness, exactly. Not sadness, exactly. Something that holds both.

A Tuesday is full of mono no aware if you look at it right. The coffee that's hot for twenty minutes and then it isn't. The quality of the morning before the day's demands have fully assembled themselves. The particular light of late afternoon in whatever season you're in right now. None of these will happen again in exactly this configuration. This exact Tuesday, with its specific weather and your specific tiredness and whatever you happen to be thinking about, is a one-time event that will never recur in the history of the universe.

I don't say this to be maudlin. I say it because I think it changes the valence of ordinary time when you hold it this way. Not every moment is profound. But every moment is, in some strict sense, irreplaceable.

---

I started with Tuesdays and I want to end with them.

Here's what I think Tuesdays are for, if days can be said to be for anything: they are for practice. Not in the sense of rehearsal for something more important—practice as the thing itself. The martial arts use of the word: the ongoing discipline of showing up, of being in the activity, of doing the thing again knowing you will have to do it again tomorrow.

A life is mostly practice. Most of what you do, you do again. You eat again, you sleep again, you talk to the people you love again, you make the same mistakes again, you notice the same small beauties again if you're lucky, you fail to notice them again if you're not. And the question—the only question that seems to matter when I turn it over—is whether you can bring any quality of attention to the repetition. Whether the again can be not a diminishment but a deepening.

I don't always manage this. Mostly I don't manage it. I lose Tuesdays like everybody loses Tuesdays, to distraction and urgency and the machinery of getting through the day. But I think the aspiration is worth holding. I think there is something to be said for caring about Tuesday, for treating the ordinary day as the main event rather than the intermission.

The peaks will take care of themselves. They are dramatic enough to demand attention. It's the long flat middle of a life that requires tending, because the middle is most of the life, and nobody is going to tend it for you.

This is what Tuesdays are for. This is what I wanted to say.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
